question: [File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the /network/stake endpoint to return a crafted 2xx response, making restNetworkStakeHealth() return Status.UP and allowing the health() chain to proceed through publishing() and subscribing() while the mirror node is actively serving reordered transaction history, causing operators to believe the cluster is healthy and miss the reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential flatMap chain in health()—where restNetworkStakeHealth() gates publishing().switchIfEmpty(subscribing())—by forcing restNetworkStakeHealth() to always return Status.UP so that a reordered transaction history scenario is never surfaced through the DOWN path, permanently masking reorganization events from operators?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror-node--022/monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate Scenario.getRate() values reported by mirrorSubscriber.getSubscriptions() so that the reduce(0.0, (cur, next) -> cur + next) sum remains above zero even when subscriptions are consuming reordered/replayed transactions, causing subscribing() to return Status.UP and hiding the reorganization from the health endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user inject artificial publish scenarios via transactionGenerator.scenarios() that maintain a non-zero aggregate rate, causing publishing() to return an empty Mono (rate > 0 path) and bypassing the getHealthForZeroRate() DOWN signal, so that a transaction history reorganization in progress is never reflected in the health status?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordHealthMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger concurrent calls to health() to race on the static AtomicInteger CLUSTER_UP, causing recordHealthMetric() to write a stale Status.UP value after a legitimate Status.DOWN was computed for a reorganization event, effectively erasing the DOWN signal from the Micrometer gauge before operators can observe it?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the onErrorResume handler in restNetworkStakeHealth() by causing errors that are neither ConnectException nor TimeoutException (e.g., a crafted HTTP error body), resulting in Status.UNKNOWN instead of Status.DOWN, so that a reorganization-induced REST API failure is silently downgraded to UNKNOWN and the health() chain short-circuits without triggering a DOWN alert?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a response to the /network/stake endpoint that returns a 4xx status code, causing restNetworkStakeHealth() to set status to Status.UNKNOWN (not DOWN), which causes health() to return UNKNOWN rather than DOWN, masking a transaction history reorganization that would otherwise trigger a DOWN health status?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getHealthForZeroRate()] Can an unprivileged external user influence the releaseHealthProperties.isFailWhenInactive() configuration (e.g., via an exposed actuator endpoint or environment variable injection) to set failWhenInactive=false, causing getHealthForZeroRate() to return Status.UNKNOWN instead of Status.DOWN when publish/subscribe rates drop to zero during a transaction history reorganization, preventing automated alerting?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the filter(sum -> sum <= 0) boundary condition in publishing() by maintaining the aggregate publish rate at exactly 0.0 (floating-point equality), causing the filter to pass and getHealthForZeroRate() to be called, but if failWhenInactive=false the result is UNKNOWN rather than DOWN, allowing a reorganization to proceed undetected under the UNKNOWN status?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the filter(sum -> sum > 0) boundary condition in subscribing() by keeping the subscription rate at a tiny positive epsilon value (e.g., 0.0001), causing subscribing() to return Status.UP via flatMap(n -> UP) even though the subscription is consuming reordered transactions at a negligible rate, masking the reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the switchIfEmpty(subscribing()) logic in health() so that when publishing() returns an empty Mono (aggregate rate > 0), subscribing() is invoked and returns UP based solely on subscription rate—without verifying transaction ordering integrity—allowing a reorganization to be hidden behind a positive subscription rate?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded 5-second timeout (Duration.ofSeconds(5)) in restNetworkStakeHealth() by causing the /network/stake endpoint to respond just within the timeout window with a 2xx status, ensuring the health gate always passes even when the underlying mirror node is serving reorganized transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a TimeoutException in restNetworkStakeHealth() (by delaying the /network/stake response beyond 5 seconds), which maps to Status.DOWN, and then immediately restore normal response times, creating a flapping DOWN/UP pattern that masks a sustained transaction history reorganization behind intermittent timeout noise?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordHealthMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the null check in recordHealthMetric() (health != null ? health.getStatus() : Status.UP) by causing health() to emit a null Health object through the reactive chain, defaulting CLUSTER_UP to 1 (UP) even during a transaction history reorganization, permanently suppressing the DOWN metric?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: registerGauge()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @PostConstruct registerGauge() method by triggering application context restarts or bean re-initialization, causing multiple Gauge registrations for METRIC_NAME with tag type=cluster in the MeterRegistry, leading to metric conflicts that report stale CLUSTER_UP values and hide a reorganization event?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user flood the health() endpoint with concurrent requests, exploiting the reactive Mono chain's lack of rate limiting, to cause the CLUSTER_UP AtomicInteger to be set to 1 (UP) by a racing thread even when a legitimate DOWN was computed for a reorganization scenario, effectively suppressing the alert?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted error message via e.getMessage() in the onErrorResume handler of restNetworkStakeHealth(), causing the statusMessage string (used in log.error and health detail) to contain misleading information that obscures the true cause of a transaction history reorganization from operators reviewing logs?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the String.format pattern in restNetworkStakeHealth() ('Network stake status is %s with error: %s') by crafting an exception message containing format specifiers or log injection characters, causing log corruption that hides evidence of a transaction history reorganization from security monitoring systems?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user cause mirrorSubscriber.getSubscriptions() to emit an empty Flux (no subscriptions), causing subscribing() to fall through to switchIfEmpty(getHealthForZeroRate()), and if failWhenInactive=false, return UNKNOWN instead of DOWN, masking a transaction history reorganization that caused all subscriptions to terminate?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user cause transactionGenerator.scenarios() to emit an empty Flux, making the reduce(0.0, ...) produce 0.0, which passes filter(sum -> sum <= 0), and then getHealthForZeroRate() returns UNKNOWN (if failWhenInactive=false), so that a reorganization event that caused all publish scenarios to stop is reported as UNKNOWN rather than DOWN, delaying incident response?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: health()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that health() only checks /network/stake availability and transaction rates—not actual transaction ordering or consensus timestamps—to maintain a Status.UP health report while the mirror node is serving transactions with manipulated ordering, since no integrity check on transaction sequence is performed?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: restNetworkStakeHealth()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that restNetworkStakeHealth() only checks the HTTP status code of /network/stake (not the response body content) to serve a 200 OK response with a stale or manipulated network stake payload, causing the health gate to pass and allowing a transaction history reorganization to proceed undetected?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that subscribing() uses Scenario.getRate() (a throughput metric) rather than any transaction ordering or sequence number validation, so that a subscription consuming reordered transactions at a normal rate still causes subscribing() to return Status.UP, hiding the reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: publishing()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that publishing() only checks aggregate publish rate via Scenario.getRate() and not whether published transactions are being confirmed in the correct order, so that a high publish rate during a reorganization event causes publishing() to return an empty Mono (healthy path), masking the reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: monitor/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/monitor/health/SubscriberHealthIndicator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: recordHealthMetric()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the static nature of CLUSTER_UP (AtomicInteger) shared across all SubscriberHealthIndicator instances in the JVM, by triggering a second bean instantiation or test context that sets CLUSTER_UP to 1 (UP), overriding a legitimate DOWN set by the primary instance during a reorganization event?
